title: The Psychology of Music
description: For questions 9 – 16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only one word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Music has (0) been an integral part of human culture for thousands of years, and scientists are increasingly interested in understanding how and why music affects us so profoundly. Research in music psychology explores the cognitive and emotional responses that music triggers.||Studies have shown that listening to music activates multiple areas (9) of the brain simultaneously, including regions associated with emotion, memory, and movement. This may explain (10) why certain songs can instantly transport us back to specific moments in our lives. The emotional power of music is remarkable – it can make us feel joy, sadness, excitement, or peace, (11) often within the span of a single composition.||Music can also have practical benefits. Research indicates that students who study (12) while listening to baroque music perform better on tests, a phenomenon known as the 'Mozart effect', although (13) the extent of this effect remains debated. Hospitals use music therapy to help patients manage pain and anxiety. Athletes often listen (14) to energising music before competitions to boost motivation and performance.||The reasons why humans are so responsive (15) to music remain partially mysterious. Some scientists believe musical ability evolved alongside language, while others argue it's a byproduct of other cognitive abilities. Whatever its evolutionary origins, there's no denying (16) that music plays a unique role in human experience, transcending cultural boundaries and connecting people across time and space.

example.text: Music has (0) an integral part of human culture for thousands of years.

questions.explanation: Expression 'areas of the brain' indicates brain regions.
Conjunction 'why' introduces an explanation about songs and memory.
Adverbio 'often' indica frecuencia de los cambios emocionales.
Temporal conjunction that introduces the moment of the study.
Definite article: 'the extent' refers to the degree of the effect.
Expression 'responsive to music' indicates sensitivity to music.
Conjunction 'that' introduces a noun clause after 'denying'.
